children.value:
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from scipy.stats import norm
np.random.seed(10)

plt.style.use('seaborn')
plt.rcParams['figure.figsize'] = (12, 8)

def plot_normal(x_range, mu=0, sigma=1, **kwargs):
    '''
    https://emredjan.github.io/blog/2017/07/19/plotting-distributions/
    '''
    x = x_range
    y = norm.pdf(x, mu, sigma)
    plt.plot(x, y, **kwargs)
    
mus = np.linspace(-6, 6, 6)
sigmas = np.linspace(1, 3, 6)

assert len(mus) == len(sigmas)
x_range = np.linspace(-10, 10, 200)

for mu, sigma in zip(mus, sigmas):
    plot_normal(x_range, mu, sigma, label=f'$\mu$ = {mu:.2f}, $\sigma$ = {sigma:.2f}')
plt.legend();

children.value: As we can see, the PDF equation

shows us how likely our sample are from a distribution with certain parameters. Current results show that our sample is more likely to have come from the first distribution. But this with just a single sample. What if we had multiple samples and we wanted to estimate the parameters?
Let us assume we have multiple samples from

children.html: XXX

children.value: which we assume to have come from some normal distribution. Also, all the samples are mutually independent of one another. In this case, the we can get the total probability of observing all samples by multiplying the probabilities of observing each sample individually.
are drawn from a normal distribution with

children.html: μ=4\mu = 4μ=4
σ=2\sigma=2σ=2

children.value: In maximum likelihood estimation (MLE), we specify a distribution of unknown parameters and then use our data to obtain the actual parameter values. In essence, MLE’s aim is to find the set of parameters for the probability distribution that maximizes the likelihood of the data points. This can be formally expressed as:

children.label: eq_likelihood
children.html: μ^,σ^=argmax⁡μ,σ∏i=1nf(xi)\hat{\mu}, \hat{\sigma} = \operatorname*{argmax}_{\mu, \sigma} \prod_{i=1}^n f(x_i)μ^​,σ^=μ,σargmax​i=1∏n​f(xi​)

children.value: However, it is difficult to optimize this product of probabilities because of long and messy calculations. Thus, we use log-likelihood which is the logarithm of the probability that the data point is observed. Formally Equation

children.label: eq_log_likelihood
children.html: μ^,σ^=argmax⁡μ,σΣi=1nln⁡f(xi)\hat{\mu}, \hat{\sigma} = \operatorname*{argmax}_{\mu, \sigma}\Sigma_{i=1}^n \ln f(x_i)μ^​,σ^=μ,σargmax​Σi=1n​lnf(xi​)

children.value: This is because logarithmic function is a monotonically increasing function. Thus, taking the log of another function does not change the point where the original function peaks. There are two main advantages of using log-likelihood:
The exponential terms in the probability density function are more manageable and easily optimizable.
The product of all likelihoods become a sum of individual likelihoods which allows these individual components to be maximized rather than working with the product of

children.html: nnn

children.value: Now, let us find the maximum likelihood estimates using the log likelihood function.

children.label: eq_mle_all_terms
children.html: ln⁡[L(μ,σ∣x1,...,xn)]=ln⁡(e−(x1−μ)2/(2σ2)σ2π×e−(x2−μ)2/(2σ2)σ2π×...×e−(xn−μ)2/(2σ2)σ2π)=ln⁡(e−(x1−μ)2/(2σ2)σ2π)+ln⁡(e−(x2−μ)2/(2σ2)σ2π)+...+ln⁡(e−(xn−μ)2/(2σ2)σ2π)\begin{align*} \begin{split} & \ln\left[ L(\mu, \sigma|x_1, ..., x_n)\right] \\ &= \ln \left( \frac{e^{-(x_1 - \mu)^{2}/(2\sigma^{2}) }} {\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \times \frac{e^{-(x_2 - \mu)^{2}/(2\sigma^{2}) }} {\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \times ... \times \frac{e^{-(x_n - \mu)^{2}/(2\sigma^{2}) }} {\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \right) \\ &= \ln\left( \frac{e^{-(x_1 - \mu)^{2}/(2\sigma^{2}) }} {\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \right) + \ln\left( \frac{e^{-(x_2 - \mu)^{2}/(2\sigma^{2}) }} {\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \right) + ... \\ &\quad + \ln\left( \frac{e^{-(x_n - \mu)^{2}/(2\sigma^{2}) }} {\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \right) \end{split} \end{align*}​ln[L(μ,σ∣x1​,...,xn​)]=ln(σ2π​e−(x1​−μ)2/(2σ2)​×σ2π​e−(x2​−μ)2/(2σ2)​×...×σ2π​e−(xn​−μ)2/(2σ2)​)=ln(σ2π​e−(x1​−μ)2/(2σ2)​)+ln(σ2π​e−(x2​−μ)2/(2σ2)​)+...+ln(σ2π​e−(xn​−μ)2/(2σ2)​)​​

ln⁡(e−(x1−μ)2/(2σ2)σ2π)=ln⁡(1σ2π)+ln⁡(e−(x1−μ)2/(2σ2))=ln⁡[(2πσ2)−12]−(x1−μ)22σ2ln⁡(e)=−12ln⁡(2πσ2)−(x1−μ)22σ2=−12ln⁡(2π)−12ln⁡(σ2)−(x1−μ)22σ2\begin{align*} &\ln\left( \frac{e^{-(x_1 - \mu)^{2}/(2\sigma^{2}) }} {\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \right) \\ &= \ln\left( \frac{1} {\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \right) + \ln\left( e^{-(x_1 - \mu)^{2}/(2\sigma^{2}) } \right) \\ &= \ln\left[ (2\pi\sigma^2)^{\frac{-1}{2}} \right] - \frac{(x_1 - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\ln(e) \\ &= -\frac{1}{2}\ln ( 2\pi\sigma^2) - \frac{(x_1-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2} \\ &= -\frac{1}{2}\ln(2\pi) - \frac{1}{2}\ln(\sigma^2) - \frac{(x_1 - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2} \\ \end{align*}​ln(σ2π​e−(x1​−μ)2/(2σ2)​)=ln(σ2π​1​)+ln(e−(x1​−μ)2/(2σ2))=ln[(2πσ2)2−1​]−2σ2(x1​−μ)2​ln(e)=−21​ln(2πσ2)−2σ2(x1​−μ)2​=−21​ln(2π)−21​ln(σ2)−2σ2(x1​−μ)2​​

children.label: eq_mle_single_term
children.html: =−12ln⁡(2π)−ln⁡(σ)−(x1−μ)22σ2\begin{align*} &= -\frac{1}{2}\ln(2\pi) - \ln(\sigma) - \frac{(x_1 - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2} \\ \end{align*}​=−21​ln(2π)−ln(σ)−2σ2(x1​−μ)2​​

ln⁡[L(μ,σ∣x1,...,xn)]=ln⁡(e−(x1−μ)2/(2σ2)σ2π)+ln⁡(e−(x2−μ)2/(2σ2)σ2π)+...+ln⁡(e−(xn−μ)2/(2σ2)σ2π)=[−12ln⁡(2π)−ln⁡(σ)−(x1−μ)22σ2]+[−12ln⁡(2π)−ln⁡(σ)−(x2−μ)22σ2]+...+[−12ln⁡(2π)−ln⁡(σ)−(xn−μ)22σ2]\begin{align*} \ln\left[ L(\mu, \sigma|x_1, ..., x_n)\right] &= \ln\left( \frac{e^{-(x_1 - \mu)^{2}/(2\sigma^{2}) }} {\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \right) + \ln\left( \frac{e^{-(x_2 - \mu)^{2}/(2\sigma^{2}) }} {\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \right) + ... \\ &\quad + \ln\left( \frac{e^{-(x_n - \mu)^{2}/(2\sigma^{2}) }} {\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \right) \\ &= \left[ -\frac{1}{2}\ln(2\pi) - \ln(\sigma) - \frac{(x_1 - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2} \right] \\ &\quad + \left[ -\frac{1}{2}\ln(2\pi) - \ln(\sigma) - \frac{(x_2- \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2} \right] \\ &\quad + ... + \left[ -\frac{1}{2}\ln(2\pi) - \ln(\sigma) - \frac{(x_n - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2} \right] \\ \end{align*}ln[L(μ,σ∣x1​,...,xn​)]​=ln(σ2π​e−(x1​−μ)2/(2σ2)​)+ln(σ2π​e−(x2​−μ)2/(2σ2)​)+...+ln(σ2π​e−(xn​−μ)2/(2σ2)​)=[−21​ln(2π)−ln(σ)−2σ2(x1​−μ)2​]+[−21​ln(2π)−ln(σ)−2σ2(x2​−μ)2​]+...+[−21​ln(2π)−ln(σ)−2σ2(xn​−μ)2​]​

children.label: eq_mle_simplified
children.html: =−n2ln⁡(2π)−nln⁡(σ)−(x1−μ)22σ2−(x2−μ)22σ2−...−(xn−μ)22σ2\begin{align*} &= -\frac{n}{2}\ln(2\pi) - n\ln(\sigma) - \frac{(x_1-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2} - \frac{(x_2-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2} - ... - \frac{(x_n-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2} \end{align*}​=−2n​ln(2π)−nln(σ)−2σ2(x1​−μ)2​−2σ2(x2​−μ)2​−...−2σ2(xn​−μ)2​​

children.value: . For this purpose, we take the partial derivative of Equation

children.html: μ\muμ
σ\sigmaσ

∂∂μln⁡[L(μ,σ∣x1,x2,...,xn)]=0−0+x1−μσ2+x2−μσ2+...+xn−μσ2\begin{align*} \frac{\partial}{\partial \mu}\ln\left[L(\mu, \sigma|x_1, x_2, ..., x_n) \right] &= 0 - 0 + \frac{x_1 - \mu}{\sigma^2} + \frac{x_2 - \mu}{\sigma^2} + ... + \frac{x_n - \mu}{\sigma^2} \\ \end{align*}∂μ∂​ln[L(μ,σ∣x1​,x2​,...,xn​)]​=0−0+σ2x1​−μ​+σ2x2​−μ​+...+σ2xn​−μ​​

children.label: eq_mu
children.html: ∂∂μln⁡[L(μ,σ∣x1,x2,...,xn)]=1σ2[(x1+x2+...+xn)−nμ]\begin{align*} \frac{\partial}{\partial \mu}\ln\left[L(\mu, \sigma|x_1, x_2, ..., x_n) \right] &= \frac{1}{\sigma^2}\left[ (x_1 + x_2 + ... + x_n) - n\mu \right] \end{align*}∂μ∂​ln[L(μ,σ∣x1​,x2​,...,xn​)]​=σ21​[(x1​+x2​+...+xn​)−nμ]​

∂∂σln⁡[L(μ,σ∣x1,x2,...,xn)]=0−nσ+(x1−μ)2σ3+(x2−μ)2σ3+...+(xn−μ)2σ3\begin{align*} \frac{\partial}{\partial \sigma}\ln\left[L(\mu, \sigma|x_1, x_2, ..., x_n) \right] &= 0 - \frac{n}{\sigma} + \frac{(x_1 - \mu)^2}{\sigma^3} + \frac{(x_2 - \mu)^2}{\sigma^3} + ... + \frac{(x_n - \mu)^2}{\sigma^3} \\ \end{align*}∂σ∂​ln[L(μ,σ∣x1​,x2​,...,xn​)]​=0−σn​+σ3(x1​−μ)2​+σ3(x2​−μ)2​+...+σ3(xn​−μ)2​​

children.label: eq_sigma
children.html: ∂∂σln⁡[L(μ,σ∣x1,x2,...,xn)]=−nσ+1σ3[(x1−μ)2+(x2−μ)2+...+(xn−μ)2]\begin{align*} \frac{\partial}{\partial \sigma}\ln\left[L(\mu, \sigma|x_1, x_2, ..., x_n) \right] &= -\frac{n}{\sigma} + \frac{1}{\sigma^3}\left[ (x_1 - \mu)^2 + (x_2 - \mu)^2 + ...+ (x_n - \mu)^2 \right] \end{align*}∂σ∂​ln[L(μ,σ∣x1​,x2​,...,xn​)]​=−σn​+σ31​[(x1​−μ)2+(x2​−μ)2+...+(xn​−μ)2]​

children.value: Now, to find the maximum likelihood estimate for

children.html: μ\muμ
σ\sigmaσ

children.value: , we need to solve for the derivative with respect to

children.html: μ=0\mu = 0μ=0
σ=0\sigma = 0σ=0

children.value: , because the slope is 0 at the peak of the curve.

children.html: ∂∂μln⁡[L(μ,σ∣x1,x2,...,xn)]=0\frac{\partial}{\partial \mu}\ln\left[L(\mu, \sigma|x_1, x_2, ..., x_n) \right] = 0∂μ∂​ln[L(μ,σ∣x1​,x2​,...,xn​)]=0

0=1σ2[(x1+x2+...+xn)−nμ]0=(x1+x2+...+xn)−nμ\begin{align*} 0 &= \frac{1}{\sigma^2}\left[ (x_1 + x_2 + ... + x_n) - n\mu \right] \\ 0 &= (x_1+x_2 + ... + x_n) - n\mu \\ \end{align*}00​=σ21​[(x1​+x2​+...+xn​)−nμ]=(x1​+x2​+...+xn​)−nμ​

children.label: eq_mu_final
children.html: μ=(x1+x2+...+xn)n\begin{align*} \mu &= \frac{(x_1+x_2+...+x_n)}{n} \end{align*}μ​=n(x1​+x2​+...+xn​)​​

children.value: Thus, the maximum likelihood estimate for

children.html: μ\muμ

children.value: is the mean of the samples.

children.html: ∂∂σln⁡[L(μ,σ∣x1,x2,...,xn)]=0\frac{\partial}{\partial \sigma}\ln\left[L(\mu, \sigma|x_1, x_2, ..., x_n) \right] = 0∂σ∂​ln[L(μ,σ∣x1​,x2​,...,xn​)]=0

0=−nσ+1σ3[(x1−μ)2+(x2−μ)2+...+(xn−μ)2]0=−n+1σ2[(x1−μ)2+(x2−μ)2+...+(xn−μ)2]nσ2=(x1−μ)2+(x2−μ)2+...+(xn−μ)2\begin{align*} 0 &= -\frac{n}{\sigma} + \frac{1}{\sigma^3}\left[ (x_1 - \mu)^2 + (x_2 - \mu)^2 + ...+ (x_n - \mu)^2 \right] \\ 0 &= -n + \frac{1}{\sigma^2}\left[ (x_1-\mu)^2 + (x_2-\mu)^2 + ...+ (x_n-\mu)^2 \right] \\ n\sigma^2 &= (x_1-\mu)^2 + (x_2-\mu)^2 + ...+ (x_n-\mu)^2 \\ \end{align*}00nσ2​=−σn​+σ31​[(x1​−μ)2+(x2​−μ)2+...+(xn​−μ)2]=−n+σ21​[(x1​−μ)2+(x2​−μ)2+...+(xn​−μ)2]=(x1​−μ)2+(x2​−μ)2+...+(xn​−μ)2​

children.label: eq_sigma_final
children.html: σ=(x1−μ)2+(x2−μ)2+...+(xn−μ)2n\begin{align*} \sigma &= \sqrt{\frac{(x_1-\mu)^2 + (x_2-\mu)^2 + ...+ (x_n-\mu)^2}{n}} \\ \end{align*}σ​=n(x1​−μ)2+(x2​−μ)2+...+(xn​−μ)2​​​

children.value: Thus, the maximum likelihood estimate for

children.html: σ\sigmaσ

children.value: is the standard deviation of the samples.

Let us now consider 5 samples with values 0, -5,  6, -9 and 8. We want to know the normal distribution from which all of these samples were most likely to be drawn. In other words, we would like to maximize the value of

children.html: f(0,−5,6,−9,8)f(0, -5, 6, -9, 8)f(0,−5,6,−9,8)

children.value: . Since we do not know the values of

children.html: μ\muμ
σ\sigmaσ

children.value: for the required distribution, we need to estimate them using  Equations

children.html: μ\muμ
σ\sigmaσ

children.value:
samples = np.array([0, -5, 6, -9, 8])
mu = np.mean(samples)
sigma = np.std(samples)
print(f'mu = {mu:.2f} and sigma = {sigma:.2f}')
Let us plot the normal distribution with these values and also mark the given points.

x_range = np.linspace(-20, 20, 200)

plot_normal(x_range, mu, sigma)
plt.axhline(y=0)
plt.vlines(samples, ymin=0, ymax=norm.pdf(samples, mu, sigma), linestyle=':')

plt.plot(samples, [0]*samples.shape[0], 'o', zorder=10, clip_on=False);
Thus, this is the most likely normal distribution from which the five sample points were drawn out.
